title: Threshold
prompt: Dusk-lit Johannesburg. A rooftop shack crammed with radios. A man's silhouette against a single bulb. His hand freezes on a dial. Static crackles, then a clear voice cuts through.
narrative: His fingers lift from the dial, leaving a greasy print. The pencil hovers over the plaster, unmarked. 91.4. The clock glows 7:03. Friday looms. The station plan was solid: water, supplies, the only rational move. He hasn't left this roof in eleven days. The voice, clear as glass, shattered the plan. He presses his palms to his knees, feeling the bone. Wind scrapes the shack. Below, a car alarm wails its thin protest. He watches the glowing 91.4. He waits.
narration: The sweep is ritual. Forty-seven to one-sixty-two megahertz, his pencil ticking the dead bands. Solder and dust perfume the air. His fingers are inked with copper. He fixed the receiver twice this week. At 91.4, the static’s texture changes—not in volume, but in structure. A pulse under the hiss. He turns back. It remains. The pulse has grammar. He adjusts the gain. Static peels away. A woman’s voice, clear and undistorted, speaks one sentence in an impossible accent. "If you can hear this, do not go to the station on Friday." The signal dies. He replays it: the pulse, the voice, the words. The clock reads 6:47. He does not move. The wind shifts. Johannesburg glows in broken light. His hand finds the dial. He returns to 91.4. Static.
